name: Jeweler
summary: A craftsperson and merchant who cuts, sets, and sells precious metals and gems—anchoring luxury trade from medieval goldsmith guilds to global flagship boutiques.
duties: Design pieces, bench-fabricate settings, appraise stones, repair heirlooms, and serve retail clients.
skills: Stone setting, soldering, gemology basics, sales, design sense.
tools: Bench pin, loupe, torch, rolling mill, display cases.
regions: Amsterdam diamond district, Jaipur, New York 47th Street, Geneva.

events.label: GIA grading standards
events.description: Standardized diamond reports reshape consumer trust and pricing transparency.

educationPath: Bench apprenticeship, GIA courses, design school.
modernSectors: Fine jewelry, repair shops, e-commerce, custom bridal.

name: Cobbler
summary: A shoemaker who repairs footwear and sometimes builds custom pairs—once a street-corner staple before mass manufacturing, still vital for quality resoling and heritage craft.
duties: Replace soles and heels, stretch leather, stitch uppers, and advise on fit.
skills: Leatherwork, lasting, customer measurement, quick turnaround.
tools: Lasts, awls, sewing machine, edge irons, polish.
regions: European villages, Middle Eastern suqs, urban repair alleys worldwide.
originLabel: Guild cordwainers and street repair stalls

events.label: Factory shoe dominance
events.description: Mass production shrinks bespoke making but sustains repair niches.

name: Potter
summary: A ceramic artisan who shapes clay into vessels and art on wheel or mold—one of humanity's oldest trades, from amphora workshops to studio pottery movements.
duties: Wedging clay, throwing or molding forms, glazing, firing kilns, and selling wares.
skills: Wheel control, glaze chemistry, kiln firing profiles, design aesthetics.
tools: Potter's wheel, kiln, ribs, glazes, pug mill.
regions: Greece, China, British studio pottery, Southwestern U.S. traditions.

events.label: Studio pottery revival
events.description: Bernard Leach and peers elevate craft pottery as fine art in the West.

name: Locksmith
summary: A security tradesworker who installs, picks, and fabricates locks and keys—evolving from warded iron mechanisms to electronic access control in modern buildings.
duties: Cut keys, rekey cylinders, open safes, install deadbolts, and program smart locks.
skills: Mechanical aptitude, lock picking ethics, blueprint reading, customer trust.
tools: Key machines, pick sets, pinning kits, drill jigs, code books.
regions: Urban service trades globally; safe specialists in financial districts.

events.label: Pin tumbler standardization
events.description: Modular cylinder systems spread through residential and commercial hardware.

modernSectors: Mobile residential, commercial access, automotive, forensic locksmithing.

name: Florist
summary: A retailer and designer who arranges cut flowers and plants for ceremonies and daily gift-giving—professionalized as refrigerated supply chains extended bloom seasons.
duties: Source stems, design bouquets, maintain coolers, deliver events, and advise on care.
skills: Color harmony, wiring techniques, seasonality, customer occasions.
tools: Cooler, floral foam, shears, ribbon, POS.
regions: Dutch auction hubs feeding global flower districts.
originLabel: Urban flower stalls and conservatory trade

events.label: Cold-chain distribution
events.description: Jet freight and Dutch auctions globalize year-round rose and lily supply.

modernSectors: Shops, weddings, corporate installs, grocery floral departments.

name: Gunsmith
summary: A precision mechanic who builds, repairs, and customizes firearms—descended from armorer guilds and still essential for hunting, sport shooting, and military maintenance.
duties: Blueprint actions, bore barrels, fit stocks, troubleshoot malfunctions, and ensure safe function.
skills: Machining, ballistics basics, wood checkering, regulatory compliance.
tools: Lathe, headspace gauges, reamers, action wrenches, bore scopes.
regions: American rural shops, European hunting estates, military arsenals.

events.label: U.S. Gun Control Act
events.description: Federal licensing reshapes who may operate commercial gunsmith businesses.

name: Bookbinder
summary: A finisher who sews signatures, attaches boards, and gilds volumes—cornerstone of publishing before perfect binding and ebooks, still prized for conservation and luxury editions.
duties: Sew text blocks, case in covers, repair spines, tool leather, and advise on archival storage.
skills: Sewing patterns, leather paring, gold tooling, paper chemistry.
tools: Sewing frame, nipping press, finishing tools, guillotine.
regions: European binderies, university conservation labs, fine press publishers.

events.label: Perfect binding dominance
events.description: Adhesive paperbacks reduce hand-binding demand except at the luxury tier.

name: Watchmaker
summary: A horologist who assembles, regulates, and restores mechanical timepieces—from parish clockmakers to Swiss atelier brands commanding collector premiums.
duties: Disassemble movements, clean oils, replace parts, test timing, and advise on service intervals.
skills: Micro-mechanics, patience, brand-specific training, customer education.
tools: Loupe, staking set, timing machine, ultrasonic cleaner.
regions: Geneva, Glashütte, Tokyo service centers, independent ateliers.

events.label: Quartz crisis
events.description: Electronic watches decimate mechanical production until luxury revival.

educationPath: WOSTEP or brand academies, micro-mechanics courses.
modernSectors: Authorized service, vintage restoration, independent haute horlogerie.

name: Goldsmith
summary: A precious-metal worker who alloys, casts, and fabricates gold jewelry and plate—guild-protected for centuries as monarchs and churches commissioned sacred and ceremonial metalwork.
duties: Alloy metals, cast ingots, forge sheet, fabricate settings, and hallmark pieces.
skills: Metallurgy, forging, filigree, assay awareness, design.
tools: Crucibles, rolling mill, draw plates, chasing tools.
regions: Benares, Florence, London Assay Office orbit, West African markets.

events.label: Hallmark statutes
events.description: English assay marks formalize purity guarantees for gold trade.

name: Silversmith
summary: An artisan who raises, casts, and engraves silver tableware and objects—defining domestic luxury in colonial America and Georgian England before stainless steel competed at table.
duties: Raise vessels, solder handles, engrave crests, repair dents, and polish finishes.
skills: Hammer raising, soldering, design proportion, antique authentication.
tools: Stakes, hammers, pitch bowl, gravers, buffing wheels.
regions: Sheffield, New England colonial shops, Mexican taxco tradition.

events.label: Electroplating
events.description: Sheffield plate and later electroplate broaden affordable hollowware markets.

name: Engraver
summary: A craftsman who incises text and imagery into metal, wood, or glass—essential for printing plates, firearms, trophies, and personalization long before laser machines.
duties: Transfer designs, cut lines by hand or machine, proof prints, and touch up burrs.
skills: Line control, typography, shading techniques, tool sharpening.
tools: Burins, gravers, pantograph, laser engraver (modern).
regions: Print shops, trophy houses, banknote bureaus, jewelry benches.

events.label: Laser engraving adoption
events.description: CAD-driven lasers supplement hand engraving in personalization shops.

name: Cabinetmaker
summary: A furniture builder who constructs case goods with joinery and veneer—distinct from rough carpentry by focus on finished interiors, drawers, and decorative wood matching.
duties: Read shop drawings, mill lumber, cut joinery, apply finishes, and install built-ins.
skills: Joinery, veneer matching, finishing, client consultation.
tools: Table saw, hand planes, domino joiner, spray booth.
regions: European ateliers, American Shaker tradition, Asian rosewood shops.

events.label: Flat-pack furniture
events.description: IKEA-style kits compete with bespoke cabinet shops in mass market segments.

name: Upholsterer
summary: A soft-goods installer who pads, springs, and fabrics seating—paired with cabinetmakers as parlors and offices demanded comfortable matched suites.
duties: Strip old covers, repair frames, tie springs, cut fabric, and install trim.
skills: Pattern cutting, sewing, ergonomics, fabric durability knowledge.
tools: Staple gun, sewing machine, webbing stretcher, button press.
regions: Furniture districts, automotive trim shops, marine interiors.

events.label: Foam rubber adoption
events.description: Polyurethane foam replaces horsehair in many residential seating lines.

name: Saddler
summary: A leatherworker specializing in horse tack, harness, and saddles—critical to cavalry, agriculture, and sport riding before automobiles yet enduring in equestrian culture.
duties: Fit saddles, stitch bridles, repair stirrup leathers, and customize tack.
skills: Leather cutting, saddle tree fitting, stitching, equine anatomy.
tools: Skiving knife, awl, saddle stand, leather punch.
regions: Mongolian steppe, American West, European dressage ateliers.

events.label: Automobile transition
events.description: Motor transport shrinks working harness demand while sport riding sustains craft.

name: Basket Weaver
summary: A maker who plaites reeds, willow, or cane into carrying and storage forms—among the oldest container crafts, still practiced in indigenous and artisan market traditions.
duties: Harvest and soak materials, weave bases and sides, finish rims, and teach workshops.
skills: Pattern rhythm, material preparation, dyeing, teaching.
tools: Awls, soaking tubs, molds, knives.
regions: Native American pueblos, African markets, European willow craft.

events.label: Craft fair revival
events.description: Farmers markets and folk festivals sustain artisan basket income streams.

name: Haberdasher
summary: A retailer of men's furnishings—hats, gloves, ties, and notions—once a distinct high-street class before department stores and fast fashion absorbed the category.
duties: Stock accessories, measure hats, advise on formal dress, and run seasonal displays.
skills: Men's style, inventory curation, personal service, gift wrapping.
tools: Hat blocks, tie racks, measuring tapes, display cases.
regions: London Jermyn Street, New York Madison Avenue historic shops.

events.label: Casual dress shift
events.description: Business casual reduces daily tie and hat demand in many markets.

name: Mercer
summary: A dealer in fine textiles and dry goods—especially silk and linen—whose shops were precursors to department stores in European commercial districts.
duties: Import fabrics, maintain sample books, extend credit to tailors, and advise on weave quality.
skills: Textile knowledge, import letters, credit judgment, fashion awareness.
tools: Sample swatches, ledgers, bolts on shelves.
regions: London Cheapside, Paris merceries, colonial port cities.

events.label: Department store rise
events.description: Bon Marché-style emporiums absorb mercer assortments under one roof.

name: Draper
summary: A cloth merchant selling yard goods by the bolt—backbone of local garment economies before ready-to-wear chains and online fabric retailers.
duties: Stock wools and cottons, cut measures, advise dressmakers, manage seasonal inventory.
skills: Fabric identification, measuring, trend spotting, bookkeeping.
tools: Bolt racks, yardsticks, scissors, swatch cards.
regions: British high streets, Indian textile bazaars, African wax-print shops.

events.label: Fast fashion ascent
events.description: Garment factories bypass home sewing and reduce yard-goods foot traffic.

name: Ironmonger
summary: A hardware retailer stocking nails, tools, hinges, and household metalware—vital village trade before big-box home centers consolidated supply.
duties: Order iron goods, advise builders, cut chain and rope, and manage credit accounts.
skills: Product breadth, practical advice, inventory management.
tools: Bins, scales, cutting tools, catalogues.
regions: British villages, colonial American general hardware.

events.label: Big-box hardware
events.description: Warehouse home centers capture DIY and contractor volume from independents.

name: Cutler
summary: A blade maker and sharpener producing knives, scissors, and edged tools—named for Sheffield and Solingen districts that industrialized steel cutlery.
duties: Forge or stamp blades, harden and temper, attach handles, sharpen and retail.
skills: Heat treat, grinding angles, ergonomics, safety standards.
tools: Grinders, forging hearth, honing stones, handle presses.
regions: Sheffield, Solingen, Seki Japan, Thiers France.

events.label: Stamped cutlery factories
events.description: Mechanized production scales household knife sets globally.

name: Pewterer
summary: A worker in tin alloy tableware—tankards, plates, and spoons—common in households before porcelain and stainless replaced pewter at everyday meals.
duties: Cast spins, turn pieces on lathe, solder handles, and polish surfaces.
skills: Alloy control, lathe work, mold making, antique repair.
tools: Spinning lathe, casting molds, solder irons.
regions: England, Germany, colonial American taverns.

events.label: Ceramic competition
events.description: Transfer-printed pottery and later stainless displace pewter from daily table use.

name: Hatter
summary: A hat maker and seller who blocked felt and straw forms—once mandatory social dress until mid-20th-century bareheads and casual caps shrank the trade.
duties: Block hats, trim ribbons, fit customers, and repair sweatbands.
skills: Blocking, steaming, style history, head measurement.
tools: Hat blocks, steam kettle, iron, display heads.
regions: London, Paris, New York millinery districts.

events.label: Hatless fashion
events.description: Presidential and cultural shifts accelerate decline of daily men's hat wearing.

name: Glover
summary: A specialist glove maker who cut leather to hand patterns—luxury accessory craft centered in Grenoble and Worcester until mass production and changing dress codes reduced demand.
duties: Grade leather, cut tranks, sew seams, pair sizes, and finish cuffs.
skills: Fine stitching, leather selection, sizing, fashion coordination.
tools: Cutting knives, sewing clams, pressing irons.
regions: France, England, Italy glove towns.

events.label: Synthetic alternatives
events.description: Nylon and later touch-screen needs reshape glove materials and markets.

name: Tobacconist
summary: A retailer of cured leaf, pipes, cigars, and later cigarettes—corner-shop fixture until health regulation and vaping shifted consumption patterns.
duties: Curate blends, maintain humidors, advise cutters, and comply with age laws.
skills: Product lore, humidity control, regulatory compliance, customer memory.
tools: Humidors, cutters, scales, POS age verification.
regions: Cuba, Virginia tobacco towns, European pipe shops.
originLabel: Colonial tobacco roll and pipe shops

events.label: Surgeon General warning
events.description: U.S. health disclosures begin decades of declining smoking prevalence.

name: Stationer
summary: A seller of paper, pens, ledgers, and office supplies—guild chartered in London to regulate the book trade's material side before big-box office chains.
duties: Stock stationery, custom print orders, advise on office setups, and manage school season spikes.
skills: Product range management, small print coordination, B2B service.
tools: Shelves, copy center, binding equipment, e-commerce catalog.
regions: University towns, business districts, suburban office retail.

events.label: Office superstore boom
events.description: Staples-style chains consolidate corporate stationery purchasing.

educationPath: Retail management, print shop crossover training.
modernSectors: Independent shops, campus stores, corporate facilities contracts.

name: Antiquarian
summary: A dealer in old books, maps, and collectible paper—scholar-merchant hybrid whose catalogs shaped historical bibliography before online marketplaces globalized rare inventory.
duties: Source at auction, catalog items, authenticate editions, and advise collectors.
skills: Bibliography, condition grading, negotiation, provenance research.
tools: Reference libraries, catalogues, climate-controlled storage, online listings.
regions: London, Paris, New York rare book trade.

events.label: ABE and online listings
events.description: Internet marketplaces connect dealers and collectors beyond print catalog mailers.

name: Clockmaker
summary: A builder of domestic and tower clocks who cut gears and escapements—parent trade to watchmaking, diminished as quartz and phone timekeeping replaced hallway pendulums.
duties: Fabricate movements, install dials, maintain tower clocks, and restore antiques.
skills: Gear cutting, escapement theory, heavy installation rigging.
tools: Lathe, depthing tool, bell ropes, lifting tackle.
regions: Black Forest, Connecticut clock valleys, church tower commissions.
originLabel: Town clock and domestic longcase shops

events.label: Electric synchronous clocks
events.description: Mains-powered clocks reduce mechanical household clock demand.

name: Optician
summary: A vision-care retailer who grinds lenses and fits eyeglasses—professionalized as refractive science and fashion frames turned spectacles into a health-and-style category.
duties: Perform fittings, edge lenses, adjust frames, sell contacts, and verify prescriptions.
skills: Facial measurement, lensometry, fashion advising, insurance billing.
tools: Edger, phoropter (with doctor), frame warmers, UV boxes.
regions: Global high streets and mall chains.

events.label: Plastic frames boom
events.description: Injection-molded frames diversify styles and lower price points.

educationPath: Opticianry degree or apprenticeship, ABO certification.
modernSectors: Retail chains, ophthalmology clinics, online try-on hybrids.

name: Brush Maker
summary: An artisan who drilled handles and knotted bristles for household, paint, and toiletry brushes—industrialized in factories yet surviving in specialty broom and art-brush niches.
duties: Select bristle, drill stock, wire or epoxy tufts, trim shapes, and quality test.
skills: Tufting, material pairing, production rhythm.
tools: Drill press, tufting machine, trimming knives.
regions: New England brush factories, German artisan brush towns.

events.label: Synthetic filament
events.description: Nylon bristles replace natural hog and horsehair in many product lines.

name: Comb Maker
summary: A craftsperson who cut teeth into horn, wood, or early celluloid for grooming—vanished as injection-molded plastic combs became disposable commodities.
duties: Saw blanks, cut fine teeth, polish, and retail at market stalls.
skills: Fine sawing, horn heating, symmetry.
tools: Saw frames, horn molds, polishing wheels.
regions: Comb-making valleys in France and New England.

events.label: Celluloid combs
events.description: Synthetic materials enable mass comb production and end artisan dominance.

name: Button Maker
summary: A manufacturer of fasteners from bone, metal, and pearl—critical garment supply before zippers and plastic buttons consolidated in distant factories.
duties: Turn blanks, drill holes, polish, and package assortments for haberdashers.
skills: Lathe work, material sourcing, quality sorting.
tools: Turning lathe, drills, tumbling barrels.
regions: Birmingham, Rhode Island button valleys.

events.label: Plastic button dominance
events.description: Bakelite and later resins replace natural materials at scale.

name: Lace Maker
summary: A textile artist who knots or bobbins delicate openwork—luxury craft from Venetian punto in aria to Belgian machine lace that competed with hand traditions.
duties: Follow patterns, repair heirloom lace, teach techniques, and sell yardage.
skills: Bobbin control, patience, pattern reading, restoration.
tools: Pillows, bobbins, pins, pattern prickings.
regions: Bruges, Honiton, Alençon lace traditions.

events.label: Bobbinet machine lace
events.description: Mechanized net undercuts hand lace in fashion trim markets.

name: Tapestry Weaver
summary: A workshop artisan who wove narrative wall hangings on upright looms—medieval prestige art sustaining ateliers like Gobelins for state and museum commissions.
duties: Interpret cartoons, blend yarns, weave slits, and repair historical pieces.
skills: Color mixing, loom setup, patience, art-history literacy.
tools: High-warp loom, bobbins, cartoon tracings.
regions: Gobelins Paris, Aubusson, medieval guild halls.
originLabel: Romanesque and Gothic wall tapestry workshops

events.label: Gobelins royal factory
events.description: Louis XIV centralizes elite tapestry production in Paris.

name: Stained Glass Artisan
summary: A glazier who cuts colored glass and leads came for windows—defining cathedral aesthetics and enduring in restoration and contemporary architectural art.
duties: Cut glass to cartoons, lead up panels, solder, cement, and install protective glazing.
skills: Glass cutting, lead came work, structural bracing, historic matching.
tools: Glass cutter, lead knife, soldering iron, light table.
regions: Gothic Europe, Arts and Crafts revival studios worldwide.

events.label: Tiffany studio peak
events.description: Art nouveau opalescent glass expands secular stained-glass markets.

name: Horn Worker
summary: A craftsperson who heated and pressed animal horn into combs, spoons, and translucent windows—supplanted by plastics and sheet glass in everyday goods.
duties: Soak horn, press in molds, polish translucent panels, and finish edges.
skills: Heat timing, mold alignment, finishing.
tools: Press molds, polishing compounds, saws.
regions: Horn comb districts of Europe and New England.
originLabel: Horn comb and lantern pane trade

events.label: Plastic substitution
events.description: Celluloid and bakelite end mass horn consumer goods.

name: Tile Setter
summary: A finisher who lays ceramic and stone surfaces on floors and walls—ancient mosaic craft scaled to suburban bathrooms and subway stations alike.
duties: Prepare substrates, cut tile, mix mortar, grout, and waterproof wet areas.
skills: Layout geometry, waterproofing, cutting, stamina.
tools: Wet saw, trowels, levels, spacers.
regions: Mediterranean mosaic sites, global construction trades.

events.label: Mass ceramic tile production
events.description: Factory-fired tiles make decorative surfaces affordable in middle-class housing.

name: Mirror Maker
summary: A gilder who backed glass with mercury or silver amalgam to create reflectors—luxury bedroom and salon fixture before float-glass mirror factories automated the process.
duties: Silver or aluminum coat glass, seal backing paint, cut sizes, and install frames.
skills: Coating chemistry, glass handling, frame joinery.
tools: Silvering baths, glass cutters, framing clamps.
regions: Venetian mirror guilds, 19th-century American factories.

events.label: Float glass mirrors
events.description: Industrial mirror plate lowers cost and spreads wall mirrors widely.

name: Musical Instrument Repairer
summary: A technician who restores woodwinds, brass, strings, and keyboards—keeping school bands and symphony orchestras playable between factory production cycles.
duties: Pad replacements, crack pinning, slide alignments, regulation, and play-testing.
skills: Acoustics, precision fitting, patience, vendor parts knowledge.
tools: Leak lights, dent balls, key clamps, tuning machines.
regions: Music districts near conservatories and school systems.

events.label: School band boom
events.description: Postwar music education expands repair shop demand across suburbs.

name: Typefounder
summary: A caster of metal printing types who sold fonts to pressmen—vanished as hot metal gave way to phototype and digital typography.
duties: Cut punches, cast matrices, sort fonts, and ship cases to printers.
skills: Punch cutting, alloy control, type design judgment.
tools: Foundry kettle, matrices, sorting racks.
regions: Garamond Paris, Caslon London, American type foundries.

events.label: Phototype transition
events.description: Film and digital fonts end commercial hot-metal typefounding.

name: Rag Picker
summary: A street collector of textile scraps sold to paper mills—grim urban survival trade that fed rag-and-bone recycling economies before municipal waste systems.
duties: Walk routes, sort fibers, sell to dealers, and avoid contamination.
skills: Material sorting, route knowledge, negotiation under poverty.
tools: Sacks, handcart, scales.
regions: 19th-century Paris, London, New York tenement districts.

events.label: Wood pulp paper
events.description: Chemical pulp reduces dependence on rag fiber for newsprint.
